museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The Vectrex arrives with its own glowing vector screen, Atari's 5200 becomes a clearer retail presence, Pole Position reaches North America, and Q*bert moves from paperwork into public arcade mythology.
museum.curatorNote: November is full of overlapping launch language. Some sources simplify the Atari 5200 to November, while October records show limited earlier appearances. This exhibit keeps both ideas visible and treats trade-show availability separately from everyday retail.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: November looks like confidence: new machines, new cabinets, and shelves preparing for Christmas.

items.title: Vectrex launches in North America
items.body: General Consumer Electronics' self-contained vector console looked unlike anything else in a living room: a built-in monochrome CRT, overlays, and glowing lines instead of blocky pixels.
items.sourceNote: Vectrex references list North American release in November 1982.

items.title: Atari 5200 becomes a wider launch story
items.body: After limited October appearances, the 5200 is often remembered as a November 1982 launch, a premium American console moment mostly observed from Britain through magazines and imports.
items.certainty: verified broad window; exact rollout complicated
items.sourceNote: Atari 5200 references list October limited availability and November-December 1982 broader launch language.

items.title: Pole Position appears at AMOA in Chicago
items.body: Namco's racer, licensed by Atari for North America, used trade-show visibility as part of its move from Japanese release to Western arcade dominance.
items.sourceNote: Pole Position sources describe the November 18-20 AMOA show before mass North American release.

items.title: Q*bert's public arcade profile grows
items.body: Although its copyright publication date sits in October, Q*bert's November AMOA presence helps explain why memories and release summaries often place it in this late-1982 arcade burst.
items.sourceNote: Q*bert sources note October publication and November AMOA sales/reporting.

items.title: Christmas software pressure begins early
items.body: Consoles, micros and arcade licences were all competing for seasonal attention. In the UK, that meant shop windows, magazine adverts and family arguments about whether a computer was educational enough.
items.sourceNote: Seasonal market context, not tied to one event.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight November-window entries, with caution around region and availability.

items.title: Vectrex
items.body: A self-contained vector console with Mine Storm built in and colour overlays for many games.
items.sourceNote: Computing History and Vectrex references list November 1982.

items.title: Mine Storm
items.body: The built-in Asteroids-like shooter that made the Vectrex playable as soon as it left the box.
items.sourceNote: Mine Storm shipped built into the Vectrex.

items.title: Pole Position
items.body: Atari's licensed North American arcade release follows the Japanese launch and AMOA showing.
items.sourceNote: Pole Position release references list November 30, 1982 for North America.

items.title: Atari 5200 launch library
items.body: Super Breakout, Star Raiders, Missile Command and Space Invaders formed the system's early identity: powerful, familiar, and still anchored to arcade memory.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive lists 5200 launch-window titles.

items.title: Q*bert
items.body: A late-1982 operator-floor arrival with an October publication trail and a November public-showing story.
items.sourceNote: October copyright publication; November AMOA context.

items.title: Time Pilot
items.body: Konami's time-jumping shooter belongs to late-1982 arcade release records, though references differ on exact timing.
items.sourceNote: Some release summaries list December; included as late-1982 arcade context.

items.title: Millipede
items.body: Atari's Centipede follow-up was part of the 1982 fixed-shooter lineage, with exact public availability harder to pin to a single month.
items.sourceNote: Millipede references list 1982; November placement is contextual.

items.title: Spectrum cassette software
items.body: The British micro scene kept expanding through tapes, listings and small adverts as Christmas approached.
items.sourceNote: No precise November title date asserted.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: November's cabinet contains unusually distinctive machines.

items.title: Vectrex
items.body: The Vectrex did not ask for the family television. It arrived as its own screen, its own glow, and its own miniature arcade promise.
items.sourceNote: Vectrex release references.

items.title: Atari 5200 SuperSystem
items.body: A premium American console with a powerful base and controversial analogue controller, entering the market as Atari tried to move beyond the 2600 without leaving it behind.
items.sourceNote: Atari 5200 launch context.

items.title: Pole Position cabinet
items.body: The racing cabinet turned hardware into a performance: steering, pedal, cabinet art and queue all part of the artefact.
items.sourceNote: Pole Position sources.

items.title: UK home micro display
items.body: For British readers, the glamorous US console war sat beside a more practical question: Spectrum, BBC, Dragon, VIC-20 or Commodore, and where would the cassette deck go?
items.sourceNote: UK microcomputer context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: November magazines stood at the mouth of Christmas: preview, compare, persuade.

items.title: Vectrex curiosity
items.headline: A console with its own screen
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for November 1982.
items.curatorNote: Vectrex was tailor-made for print fascination: a machine that looked strange even before it was switched on.
items.body: Vectrex was tailor-made for print fascination: a machine that looked strange even before it was switched on.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

items.title: C&VG Christmas comparison
items.headline: Arcades, consoles and micros before Christmas
items.curatorNote: For UK players, C&VG helped translate overseas console news into local desire.
items.body: For UK players, C&VG helped translate overseas console news into local desire.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

items.title: Popular Computing Weekly
items.headline: The weekly pulse before the buying season
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for November 1982.
items.curatorNote: Weekly computing papers made the market feel faster than the machines themselves.
items.body: Weekly computing papers made the market feel faster than the machines themselves.
items.sourceNote: Specific issue line not asserted.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: The micro as household investment
items.curatorNote: The games buyer and the serious computer buyer were often the same family conversation in different voices.
items.body: The games buyer and the serious computer buyer were often the same family conversation in different voices.
items.sourceNote: Specific cover line not asserted.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: November's information system was seasonal and physical.

items.title: Christmas lists became databases
items.body: Players collected prices, adverts and rumours into mental spreadsheets long before anyone called that browsing.

items.title: Trade shows reached homes through print
items.body: AMOA and American launch news became UK knowledge only after magazines and import gossip translated it.

items.title: Comparison shopping was slow
items.body: A new console might be known from a paragraph weeks before anyone nearby had touched one.

items.title: Computer clubs filled gaps
items.body: For micro owners, local clubs and school rooms could provide the demonstrations magazines could only describe.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: November was the month of wanting machines you had not yet seen in person.

items.title: Vectrex felt impossible
items.body: A console with its own vector screen sounded like a small arcade cabinet that had escaped into the house.

items.title: Atari still looked enormous
items.body: Even when the 5200 was distant from UK shelves, Atari's decisions felt like news from the centre of the industry.

items.title: The arcade had not peaked emotionally
items.body: Pole Position and Q*bert made cabinets feel like they still owned the future.

items.title: Christmas sharpened every choice
items.body: A cartridge, a micro, a cassette, a joystick: every object had to justify itself under family scrutiny.
